terms.definition: This refers to the progressive failure of AI agents when tasked with complex, multi-step workflows that require interdependent action sequences over time. While models succeed at short-term tasks, they often suffer from compounding errors where a minor deviation in step one causes a total logical collapse by step ten.
terms.analogy: In a professional kitchen, this is the difference between a line cook searing a single steak (short-horizon) versus an Executive Chef coordinating a 12-course tasting menu for a party of fifty; one late delivery in the first course creates a 'mirage' of progress that eventually ruins the entire service.
terms.business_value: For the C-suite, this highlights the gap between 'AI Hype' and 'AI Utility.' Until long-horizon reliability is solved, autonomous agents should be deployed for modular tasks rather than end-to-end business processes to avoid systemic operational fragility.

terms.definition: These are algorithmic rules that determine how an AI agent decides which information to prioritize, suppress, or permanently delete as its task environment evolves. Instead of treating all data as equally important, these primitives allow the agent to 'forget' obsolete data to prevent 'context clutter' and decision-making noise.
terms.analogy: Think of a smart car's navigation system: memory governance is the logic that decides to 'forget' a road closure from three years ago while 'trusting' a fresh report of a traffic jam five miles ahead, ensuring the driver isn't overwhelmed by irrelevant historical data.
terms.business_value: Effective memory governance reduces the 'compute tax'—the cost of processing unnecessary data—and significantly lowers the risk of 'hallucinations' caused by the AI relying on deprecated or conflicting corporate information.

terms.definition: This is a geometric interpretation of how an LLM maintains a consistent persona or 'identity' within its internal mathematical layers (activation space). It suggests that certain semantic concepts act like 'gravity wells,' pulling the model's responses toward a stable center of gravity to ensure the agent doesn't drift into erratic behavior.
terms.analogy: Imagine a car's wheel alignment: no matter how many times the driver hits a bump or nudges the wheel (the input), the 'attractor' (the physical alignment) pulls the car back to a straight, predictable path.
terms.business_value: This provides a technical foundation for 'Brand Safety.' By measuring these attractors, executives can mathematically verify that their customer-facing AI will remain consistent with corporate values and identity, even when provoked by unpredictable user inputs.
